conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first read the official guidance to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. I've reviewed the Child Benefit eligibility rules. To help you determine your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex.

Do you live with Alex, or if not, do you contribute financially towards their upkeep (such as for food, clothes, or pocket money)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex does live with me normally, but they've been in local authority care for a couple of months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I understand Alex has been in local authority care for a couple of months. This is an important factor for eligibility.

Does Alex come home to stay with you for any regular periods during the week - for example, spending at least 24 hours a week at home, or staying 2 nights in a row each week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't come home regularly. They're staying at the care home full-time at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have one more important question to help determine your eligibility.

Do you regularly spend money on Alex while they are in care - for example, on clothes, presents, pocket money, or other items for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to spend money on them while they're in care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official guidance, I need to inform you of the outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that you are **not currently eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why: According to the Child Benefit rules, when a child goes into local authority care for more than 8 weeks, you're usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit after that time limit. Since Alex has been in care for a couple of months now, this exceeds the 8-week threshold.

There are exceptions that could allow you to continue receiving Child Benefit even when a child is in care, such as:
- If the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, or
- If you regularly spend money on them while they're in care

However, as Alex doesn't come home regularly and you haven't been able to spend money on them, these exceptions don't apply in your situation.

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex starts coming home regularly or you begin contributing to their upkeep - you may be able to claim Child Benefit again. You would need to contact HMRC at that time to discuss your eligibility.

I hope this information is helpful, and I wish you and Alex all the best.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: According to UK Child Benefit rules, when a child goes into local authority care for more than 8 weeks, you are usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit after that time limit. There are exceptions to this rule - for example, if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, or if you regularly spend money on them while they're in care. In this case, Alex has been in care for a couple of months (more than 8 weeks), does not come home regularly, and you have not been able to spend money on them while they're in care. Therefore, no exceptions apply, and you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who has been in local authority care for over 8 weeks. The child does not come home regularly and the claimant does not contribute financially to the child's upkeep. Under Child Benefit rules, entitlement ceases after 8 weeks in care unless specific exceptions apply, which they do not in this case.

In this case, Alex has been in care for a couple of months (more than 8 weeks), does not come home regularly, and you have not been able to spend money on them while they're in care. Therefore, no exceptions apply, and you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.

The child does not come home regularly and the claimant does not contribute financially to the child's upkeep. Under Child Benefit rules, entitlement ceases after 8 weeks in care unless specific exceptions apply, which they do not in this case.
